name: NavDataLayer
tagline: Screens stop hardcoding data: they observe a repository through a ViewModel and render Loading/Empty/Error/Success.
whatItDoes: You open the app to a planet list that first shows a centered spinner ('Loading planets...') for about 1.2 seconds, because the in-memory repository adds a fake delay. Then it fills with six planets (Mercury through Saturn), each row showing a title and a one-line blurb. Tapping a row opens a detail screen that briefly shows its OWN smaller spinner, then displays that planet's title, blurb, and a 'Fun fact', plus a 'Back to list' button. A teaching banner pinned to the top of the list reminds you the screen owns no data of its own. The list screen can also display Empty and Error states (with a Retry button), which the @Preview functions show off even though the live in-memory repo never actually produces them.
mentalModel: Data lives behind a repository interface, and the UI just reacts to explicit states; you can swap the data source (memory to Room to Retrofit) without changing a single screen.

screens.name: PlanetListScreen
screens.role: Stateful wrapper that grabs PlanetsViewModel, observes its uiState StateFlow, and forwards the plain state down to the body.

screens.name: PlanetListScreenBody
screens.role: Stateless body that renders purely from a PlanetsUiState via an exhaustive 'when' (Loading spinner, Empty note, Error+Retry, Success list).

screens.name: PlanetDetailScreen
screens.role: Loads one planet by id with a suspend repo call inside LaunchedEffect, showing its own spinner, then the title/blurb/fact or a 'not found' message.

screens.name: DataLayerBanner
screens.role: A tinted Surface header pinned above the list that spells out the data-boundary lesson so it cannot be missed.

concepts.plain: 'PlanetRepository' is a contract that names WHAT data exists ('planets()' and 'planet(id)') but hides HOW it is fetched. The ViewModel depends only on this interface, never on a concrete class.

'InMemoryPlanetRepository' is one concrete implementation backed by the hardcoded 'samplePlanets' list. Room or Retrofit versions would implement the same interface, so wiring one in is a one-line change.

'PlanetsUiState' is a sealed interface with exactly four subtypes: Loading, Empty, Error(message), Success(planets). Because the set is closed, a 'when' over it is exhaustive and the compiler forces the screen to handle every case.
The ViewModel keeps a private '_uiState' (MutableStateFlow) and exposes a read-only 'uiState' via '.asStateFlow()', so the UI can read the current state but never write it.

'observePlanets()' chains 'repo.planets().onEach { ... }.catch { ... }.launchIn(viewModelScope)' to map each emitted list into Empty/Success, turn upstream errors into Error, and auto-cancel when the ViewModel is cleared.

The list screen observes the StateFlow with 'collectAsStateWithLifecycle()', which pauses collection while the screen is stopped in the background; this is the lifecycle-aware alternative to plain 'collectAsState()'.

'PlanetListScreen' (stateful: holds the ViewModel) feeds a plain state into 'PlanetListScreenBody' (stateless: no ViewModel, no Flow). The stateless body is what every @Preview drives directly, which makes it trivially previewable and testable.
The detail screen calls the 'suspend' 'repo.planet(planetId)' inside a 'LaunchedEffect(planetId)', tracking 'loading' and a nullable 'planet' to show a spinner, the details, or a 'not found' message.

files.role: The UI layer: nav keys, the Activity, the NavDisplay host, the list screen (stateful + stateless split), the detail screen, and the @Preview functions for every state.
The UI-state layer: defines the sealed PlanetsUiState and the PlanetsViewModel that collects the repository Flow and maps it into state.
The data layer: the Category/Item models, the sample planets, the PlanetRepository interface (the data boundary), and the InMemoryPlanetRepository implementation.

highlights.title: The data boundary: the repository interface
highlights.explain: 'PlanetRepository' is the entire lesson in one declaration. 'planets()' returns a 'Flow<List<Item>>' (a stream that can re-emit when data changes), while 'planet(id)' is a one-shot 'suspend' read returning null when missing. The UI side depends only on this interface, so any class honoring this contract (memory, Room, Retrofit) drops in without touching a screen.

highlights.title: The in-memory implementation and its swap point
highlights.explain: 'planets()' uses the 'flow { ... }' builder to make a COLD stream: 'delay(fakeLatencyMillis)' then 'emit(samplePlanets)' only run once a collector starts. The fake delay exists purely so the Loading state is visible. The comments mark this as the ONLY method a Room or Retrofit source would change, and show how to demo the Empty (emit emptyList) or Error (throw) states.

highlights.title: The sealed UI state
highlights.explain: 'PlanetsUiState' is a 'sealed interface' with four subtypes: Loading and Empty are 'data object' singletons, while Error carries a 'message' and Success carries the 'planets' list. Because the subtype set is closed at compile time, a 'when' over this type is exhaustive, so the screen is forced to handle every state and cannot silently forget the spinner or the error case.

highlights.title: Mapping the repository Flow into state
highlights.explain: 'observePlanets()' is the heart of the ViewModel. 'repo.planets()' supplies the Flow; '.onEach' publishes Empty or Success per emitted list; '.catch' converts any upstream exception into Error (since try/catch cannot wrap a Flow); '.launchIn(viewModelScope)' collects on a scope that auto-cancels when the ViewModel dies, so no coroutine leaks. Note that writes go to the private '_uiState'.

highlights.title: Observing state the lifecycle-aware way
highlights.explain: The stateful 'PlanetListScreen' obtains a lifecycle-scoped 'PlanetsViewModel' via 'viewModel()', then observes 'viewModel.uiState.collectAsStateWithLifecycle()' which pauses collection while the screen is backgrounded. It holds no data itself: it just forwards the plain 'uiState' and 'viewModel::retry' down to the stateless body. This stateful/stateless split is what makes the body previewable.

highlights.title: Exhaustive state rendering and row taps
highlights.explain: A single 'when (uiState)' renders each branch: Loading shows a spinner, Empty a note, Error smart-casts to read 'uiState.message' plus a Retry 'Button(onClick = onRetry)', and Success smart-casts to '.planets' and builds a LazyColumn. Each row is '.clickable { onOpen(planet.id) }', which pushes a DetailKey carrying only the id, so navigation never carries the whole object.

newVsPrev: NavViewModelState introduced the ViewModel + StateFlow holding UI state that survives rotation. NavDataLayer adds a real DATA LAYER beneath that ViewModel: a 'PlanetRepository' interface (the data boundary) with a swappable 'InMemoryPlanetRepository', the data now arrives as a 'Flow' that is mapped with 'onEach'/'catch'/'launchIn', and the UI state grows into a full sealed 'PlanetsUiState' with Loading/Empty/Error/Success plus a Retry path. It also shows the detail screen re-fetching its own planet by id through a 'suspend' repo call, proving the repository (not the nav key) is the source of truth.
tryThis: In Repository.kt's 'planets()' (around line 179), replace 'emit(samplePlanets)' with 'emit(emptyList())' and rerun to watch the list screen flip to the Empty state.

In that same 'planets()' method, add 'throw IllegalStateException("Simulated data-source failure")' after the delay and rerun to see the Error state and the Retry button (then tap Retry to watch it loop back through Loading).
Change 'fakeLatencyMillis' (line 155) from 1200L to something like 4000L and notice the Loading spinner stays on screen much longer, since that delay is the only thing making Loading visible.
Add a seventh planet to 'samplePlanets' in Repository.kt and confirm it appears in the list and opens correctly in the detail screen with no other code changes.

Open the four @Preview functions at the bottom of MainActivity.kt in Android Studio's Design view and observe how each one freezes PlanetListScreenBody in a different state without ever running a ViewModel or coroutine.
gotchas: The Room and Retrofit repositories are only commented SIGN-POSTS at the bottom of Repository.kt; this project ships no Room or Retrofit dependencies, so 'InMemoryPlanetRepository' is the only implementation actually wired up.

The Empty and Error states never occur during a normal run, because the in-memory repo always emits the full 'samplePlanets' list successfully. You only see those states by editing the repository (see tryThis) or in the @Previews.

The detail screen deliberately does NOT use a ViewModel: it runs an in-place 'suspend' load inside a 'LaunchedEffect' with local 'remember' state for brevity. Only the list screen demonstrates the full ViewModel + StateFlow path, so its state is the one that survives rotation.
The 'Category' model, 'sampleCategories', and 'categoryById()' exist in the data layer but are not actually shown on any screen in this project; only 'Item' (the planet) is rendered.
runTest: Run './gradlew installDebug' from inside the NavDataLayer/ folder (or press Run in Android Studio) and launch the app. Watch the list screen show the 'Loading planets...' spinner for about 1.2 seconds, then a list of six planets under the 'Data-layer demo' banner. Tap any planet (for example Jupiter) and confirm the detail screen shows its own brief 'Loading planet...' spinner before displaying the title, blurb, and 'Fun fact', with a 'Back to list' button. Filter Logcat by the tag 'DataLayer' to see the 'Entered AppNavigation' breadcrumb. This project defines no deep links, so there is no adb command to run.
